coredata.dc:title: Impact of carbon quantum dots on dynamic properties of BSA and BSA/DPPC adsorption layers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Lu
Wei, Xiao-Qian
Huang, Wei-Hua
Mei, Ping
Ren, Zhao-Hua
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the dynamic properties of bovine serum albumin (BSA) were investigated using pendant drop profile analysis method. Moreover, the effects of CQDs on the competitive adsorption of BSA and dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine (DPPC) were examined. CQDs reduce the fluorescence intensity of BSA and cause a red shift in fluorescence emission. The quenching constant at pH 4.3 is almost twice as large as that of the value obtained at pH 6.0. A small amount of CQDs does not influence the dynamic surface adsorption properties of BSA molecules. As the CQD concentration increases, a gradual increase in adsorption rate of BSA molecules is observed. Moreover, the addition of CQDs results in a significant transition of kinetic dependencies of surface elasticity of BSA solution when the CQD concentration exceeds a critical value. The appearance of the maximum surface elasticity value is probably attributed to the formation of tails and loops. When the dynamic surface properties are dominated by BSA molecules, the effects of CQDs on the surface properties of BSA/DPPC mixture are similar to those of BSA alone. However, when the surface film mainly consists of DPPC, CQDs can obviously change the interfacial properties of DPPC monolayer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dynamic surface tension
Dynamic dilational rheology
Protein
Carbon quantum dots
Air–water interface

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fluorescence quenching of BSA induced by CQDs
Dynamic dilational properties of BSA/CQD mixtures at the air–water interface
The effects of CQDs on the DST and dynamic dilational properties of BSA/DPPC system at the air–water interface
The interaction mechanism between CQDs and BSA or BSA/DPPC at the air–water interface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of as-prepared CQDs. (a) UV–vis absorption (green line), fluorescence excitation (red line, λ em =453nm), and emission (blue line, λ ex =360nm) spectra of as-prepared CQDs. Inset: Photograph of CQD solution taken under 365nm UV light. (b) TEM image of as-prepared CQDs. Inset: the corresponding size distribution of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA solution at pH = 4.3 (a) and pH = 6.0 (b) in the presence of different concentrations of CQDs. Concentrations of CQDs were 0 to 0.54g/L at an interval of 0.06g/L, [BSA]=1μmol·L−1. The inset shows Stern–Volmer plot of the quenching of the fluorescence of BSA by CQDs, T =298.15K.

(a) Kinetic dependencies of DST of BSA solution (1×10−3, 1×10−5, 1×10−7 mol·L−1) at pH 6.0, T =298.15K; (b) Kinetic dependencies of DST of CQDs (black squares, 0.054g·L−1), BSA (red circles, 0.1μmol·L−1), and BSA/CQD mixtures at pH 6.0 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024, 0.036 and 0.054g·L−1, respectively), T =298.15K; (c) Kinetic dependencies of DST of BSA (black squares, 0.1μmol·L−1) and BSA/CQD mixtures at pH 4.3 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024 and 0.030g·L−1, respectively), T =298.15K. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Kinetic dependencies of the surface dilational elasticity of BSA (black squares, 0.1μmol·L−1) and CQDs/BSA mixtures at pH 6.0 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024, 0.036 and 0.054g·L−1, respectively), ω
= 0.1Hz, T =298.15K; (b) Kinetic dependencies of the surface dilational elasticity of BSA (black squares, 0.1μmol·L−1) and CQDs/BSA mixtures at pH 4.3 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024 and 0.030g·L−1, respectively), ω
= 0.1Hz, T =298.15K.

(a) Dynamic surface dilational elasticity as a function of surface pressure for BSA solution (black squares, 0.1μmol·L−1) and CQDs/BSA mixtures at pH 6.0 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024, 0.036 and 0.054g·L−1, respectively); (b) Dynamic surface dilational elasticity as a function of surface pressure for BSA solution (black squares, 0.1μmol·L−1) and CQDs/BSA mixtures at pH 4.3 (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c CQDs =0.006, 0.012, 0.018, 0.024 and 0.030g·L−1, respectively).

(a) Kinetic dependencies of the dynamic surface tension of BSA (10μmol·L−1), DPPC (0.67g·L−1), BSA/DPPC mixture (c BSA =10μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1), and BSA/DPPC/CQDs mixture (c BSA =10μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1, c CQDs =1.2g·L−1) at pH 6.0, T =298.15K; (b) Kinetic dependencies of the dynamic surface tension of BSA (0.1μmol·L−1), DPPC (0.67g·L−1), BSA/DPPC mixture (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1), and BSA/DPPC/CQDs mixture (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1, c CQDs =1.2g·L−1) at pH 6.0, T =298.15K; (c) The effects of CQDs (c CQDs =0.5, 1 and 1.5g·L−1, respectively) on the mesoequilibrium surface tension of DPPC (0.67g·L−1).

(a) Kinetic dependencies of surface dilational elasticity of BSA (10μmol·L−1), DPPC (0.67g·L−1), BSA/DPPC mixture (c BSA =10μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1), and BSA/DPPC/CQDs mixture (c BSA =10μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1, c CQDs =12g·L−1) at pH 6.0, ω
= 0.1Hz, T =298.15K; (b) Kinetic dependencies of surface dilational elasticity of BSA (0.1μmol·L−1), DPPC (0.67g·L−1), BSA/DPPC mixture (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1), and BSA/DPPC/CQDs mixture (c BSA =0.1μmol·L−1, c DPPC =0.67g·L−1, c CQDs =12g·L−1) at pH 6.0, ω
= 0.1Hz, T =298.15K.
Schematic diagrams of the effects of CQDs on dynamic properties of BSA and BSA/DPPC adsorption layers.

head.ce:title: Impact of carbon quantum dots on dynamic properties of BSA and BSA/DPPC adsorption layers

Lai); State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China (Y. sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the dynamic properties of bovine serum albumin (BSA) were investigated using pendant drop profile analysis method. Moreover, the effects of CQDs on the competitive adsorption of BSA and dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine (DPPC) were examined. CQDs reduce the fluorescence intensity of BSA and cause a red shift in fluorescence emission. The quenching constant at pH 4.3 is almost twice as large as that of the value obtained at pH 6.0. A small amount of CQDs does not influence the dynamic surface adsorption properties of BSA molecules. As the CQD concentration increases, a gradual increase in adsorption rate of BSA molecules is observed. Moreover, the addition of CQDs results in a significant transition of kinetic dependencies of surface elasticity of BSA solution when the CQD concentration exceeds a critical value. The appearance of the maximum surface elasticity value is probably attributed to the formation of tails and loops. When the dynamic surface properties are dominated by BSA molecules, the effects of CQDs on the surface properties of BSA/DPPC mixture are similar to those of BSA alone. However, when the surface film mainly consists of DPPC, CQDs can obviously change the interfacial properties of DPPC monolayer.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of nanotechnology increases the likelihood of nanoparticle contact with humans and the environment [1]. The small size of nanoparticles gives the material higher surface free energy than the bulk material itself [2]. This condition means that nanoparticles will immediately adsorb biomolecules (e.g., proteins, DNA, and lipids) when they are introduced into biological fluids [3]. The interaction of these biomolecules with nanoparticles is a risk to human health [4].

Many reports are investigating the interaction of nanoparticles with biomolecules in the bulk phase [5–7]. Thereinto, the interaction between quantum dots (QDs) and human serum albumin (HSA) in the bulk phase was studied most extensively [8–10]. The main research methods include fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD), cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), ζ-potential, and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). Xiao et al. investigated the interaction of different-sized CdTe QDs with HSA [11]. The interaction mechanism, affinity constants, and corresponding thermodynamic parameters were obtainedthrough fluorescence quenching and UV-vis absorption spectra. Moreover, results of CD spectra confirmed the conformational changes of HSA. In another report, the stoichiometry of HSA adsorbed to the QD surface was determined using DLS and ζ-potential [12]. However, these recent studies largely focused on the interaction mechanism between nanoparticles and proteins in the bulk phase [13,14].

The surface adsorption of biomolecules and their behavior in the interfacial adsorption layers has major biological (e.g., membranes and serum lipoproteins) and technological (e.g., food systems) significance. The behavior of biomolecules at interfaces is becoming a significant issue in pharmacological science, food, and biotechnology industries [15–17]. For example, Noskov et al. investigated the unfolding of bovine serum albumin (BSA) molecules at surfaces using dynamic dilational rheological measurements [18]. The researchers also confirmed that denaturants (e.g., urea or guanidine hydrochloride) dramatically affect the surface properties of proteins, and such effect can be attributed to the conformational transitions of proteins [19]. The knowledge on the properties of protein adsorption layers have greatly advanced in recent years [20]. Moreover, the effects of surfactants and denaturants on the properties of protein adsorption layers have been extensively investigated [21–25].

According to current reports, the important aspects of protein adsorption include the capability to reduce the interfacial tension, the conformational change in proteins during the adsorption process, the adsorbed amount of proteins, the structure of the adsorbed layer, dilational elasticity, and viscosity of protein film [20,26]. Dynamic surface tension (DST) enables us to obtain adsorption kinetics of proteins. Moreover, Pereira et al. discovered that the dilational surface elasticity of proteins are sensitive to the intraprotein structural rigidity [27]. In addition, the air–water interface is a good model for biomaterial-blood interfaces because of the hydrophobic-hydrophilic properties. Thus, studies on the biomaterial-blood interfaces are important in biotechnology.

Serum albumins are the most abundant constituent proteins in mammalian blood [28]. Serum albumins play important biological roles, including maintaining osmotic pressure and blood pH, and transporting numerous endogenous (e.g., metabolites) and exogenous materials (e.g., drugs and lipophilic compounds) [29]. BSA has 80% homology with HSA [30]. Thus far, BSA is one of the most investigated proteins. BSA molecules have five main conformations: normal form (N), which is near the isoelectric point and up to neutral pH; fast migrating (F) at pH 4; extended (E) at below pH 3; basic (B) near pH 8; and aged (A) near pH 10 [27]. The N form has a heart-like shape, whereas the F form has longer and less compact structure. At pH values below 4, BSA molecules expand into E form. Under alkaline conditions, BSA molecules undergo N-B-A conformational transitions with increasing pH value [27].

Pulmonary surfactants, which are mixtures of lipids and proteins, stabilize the lung alveoli by lowering the surface tension [31]. In lung injury, serum proteins are secreted into the alveolar lining layer. This condition inhibits the surface function of pulmonary surfactants and results in acute respiratory distress syndrome [31]. Franses et al. investigated the competitive adsorption of pulmonary lipids, namely, dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC), and serum proteins, namely, BSA at the surface [32]. They demonstrated BSA is displaced by DPPC at the interface. In addition, Rodriguez Patino et al. also found that protein is displaced by monostearin at the interface. The interaction between protein and lipid depends on protein concentration and the composition of bulk solution [33]. In another studies, Rodriguez Patino et al. found that DPPC monolayers are penetrated by beta-casein using atomic force microscopy. They observed the displacement of beta-casein from the interface by DPPC and the presence of multilayers of beta-casein and DPPC of high thickness [34].

In recent years, the rapid development of nanotechnology inevitably leads to the generation of many toxicological effects. Among these effects, the interaction between nanomaterial and cell membrane attracts more attention. Our previous study demonstrated that the adsorption of QDs on the surface of cell membrane disrupts the structure of cell membrane, and thus disturbs the function of cell membrane [35]. Moreover, Guzmán et al. investigated the interaction of DPPC with different nanoparticles at the air–water interface using Langmuir trough and Brewster angle microscopy. They found that the incorporation of nanoparticles into the DPPC monolayer changes the interfacial structure and influences the phase behavior and mechanical properties of DPPC monolayer [36,37]. Meanwhile, nanoparticles transfer from the sub-phase to the lipid monolayer, resulting from the interaction between lipid molecules and nanoparticles [38–43]. However, investigation about the dynamic interfacial properties of protein and protein/lipid mixtures at the air–water interface in the presence of nanoparticles was limited.

Recently, compared with traditional semiconductor QDs, carbon QDs (CQDs) have attracted considerable attention because of their outstanding electronic and optical properties and biocompatibility [44–46]. Therefore, the application of CQDs in drug delivery, medical imaging, and gene therapy has rapidly expanded, inevitably introducing CQDs into complex biological fluids [47]. Consequently, the effects of CQDs on the interfacial properties of proteins, as well as the properties of protein films, should be investigated in detail. In this study, we characterized the effects of CQDs on the dynamic surface properties of BSA solution at different pH values using the pendant drop profile analysis method. Moreover, the effects of CQDs on the interfacial properties of BSA/DPPC mixtures were investigated.

BSA was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. DPPC (99%) was obtained from Avanti Polar Lipids. Citric acid (99.5%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). BSA solutions were prepared in a phosphate buffer (PB, 0.02M of NaH2PO4 and NaHPO4). The pH values of the protein solution were adjusted using HCl solution (1mol·L−1). DPPC was added in a small amount of chloroform (1 mM). The DPPC solution was evaporated under reduced pressure. The obtained DPPC films were placed in a vacuum drying oven at 35°C to remove trace solvent. The DPPC films were dispersed in a PB solution, then heated and stirred for 1h at 55°C. To reduce the vesicle size, the DPPC dispersion was sonicated at 150W ultrasonic power at 55 °C until it was clear. The as-prepared DPPC dispersion was stable within one month.

CQDs were prepared following the procedure of Chi et al. [48]. CQD suspensions were purified through dialysis against PB solution for 24h. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2540 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Observations using high resolution transmission electron microscopy were performed through a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (200kV, JEOL, Japan).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a LS-55 fluorophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Corporation, USA) equipped with a thermostatic bath. The excitation and emission wavelengths for BSA were 280 and 430nm, and the widths of the excitation and emission slits were set as 5.0 and 10.0nm. Titrations were performed manually through a microinjector.

DST was examined using a commercial bubble profile tensiometer (Tracker, Teclis-IT Concept, France). Temperature was controlled at 298.15±0.1K using a thermostatic bath. In this instrument, the air–water interface was created by injecting 5μL of air into an inverse stainless steel needle attached to a gastight syringe. The tip of the bent needle was immersed in a quartz cuvette containing the protein solution. The image of the bubble was captured through a charge-coupled device camera and analyzed through a software employing the Laplace equation.

The surface dilational viscoelasticity was also measured using an automated bubble profile tensiometer (Tracker, Teclis-IT Concept, France). A new bubble was produced, and the initial volume of the bubble was 5μL. Surface rheological measurements were performed by oscillating the bubble volume to a periodic, small sinusoidal deformation of 10% of the original drop volume with a dilational frequency of 0.1Hz. The effects of CQDs on the interfacial dilational properties were studied.

The interfacial dilational modulus (ε) is defined as the ratio of a small change in interfacial tension (γ) to a specific change in interfacial area (A) [49].
(1)
ε =
d γ
d ln A

Dilational modulus (ε) can also be expressed as a complex number [49],
(2)
ε =
ε
d
+ i ω
η
d
where ε d is the dilational elasticity representing the elastic energy storage in the surface, and ε η is the dilational viscosity accounting for the energy loss in the relaxation process [49].

CQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid according to Ref. [48]. The emission wavelength of CQDs is nearly excitation-independent, implying that both the size and the surface state of the sp2 clusters contained in CQDs are uniform [50]. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the maximal emission wavelength of the as-prepared CQDs appears at 453nm when the excitation wavelength is 360nm. Moreover, the as-prepared CQDs exhibit a well-defined absorption band at 365nm. Fig. 1(b) shows that CQDs are well dispersed in narrow size distributions with average diameters of 1.59±0.3nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence quenching of BSA induced by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A variety of molecular interactions, such as excited-state reactions, complex formation, energy transfer and collision, induce the fluorescence quenching [28]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching method was employed extensively to investigate the interaction of small molecules with biomacromolecules in the bulk phase [30]. In Fig. 2 , the fluorescence emission intensity of BSA progressively decreases as CQD concentration increases. Moreover, a slightly red shift in fluorescence emission is observed, as caused by the decrease in the hydrophobicity of microenvironment around tryptophan residues. The addition of CQDs induces the slightly conformational change of BSA molecules. As a result, tryptophan residues previously buried in a hydrophobic region are exposed to a more hydrophilic environment [8].

In general, the decrease in fluorescence intensity of BSA can be fitted very well using the Stern-Volmer equation [51], which is as follows:
(3)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher, and [Q] is the quencher concentration. Quenching constant (K sv) can be obtained through linear regression of a plot of F 0/F against [Q]. The Stern–Volmer plots of the fluorescence quenching of BSA by CQDs are shown in the inset of Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2, when the CQD concentration ranges from 0g·L−1 to 0.54g·L−1, the calculated K sv for BSA at pH 4.3 is 4.8517L·g−1, which is almost twice as large as that of BSA at pH 6.0 (2.8182L·g−1). At pH values below the isoelectric point of BSA (pH=4.3), the net charge of BSA molecules is positive. The electrostatic attraction between the positively charged regions of BSA molecules (F form) and negatively charged CQDs results in the formation of BSA/CQD complex. However, at pH 6.0, BSA molecules in N form and CQDs display similar charges. Despite the negative net charge of BSA molecules (N form), the interaction between the positively charged regions of BSA molecules and CQDs cannot be neglected. But the number of positively charged groups will decrease obviously at pH 6.0 [52]. Therefore, compared with the N form, CQDs cause a more pronounced fluorescence quenching of BSA with F form.

When a fresh surface is formed, adsorption causes surface-active proteins to diffuse from the bulk solution to the surface. The general adsorption process is considered to be a three-step process. The first step is the diffusional transport of protein molecules from bulk solution to the subsurface region. Next, the protein molecules adsorb from subsurface to the surface. The final step is the conformational rearrangement of adsorbed protein molecules [15]. As protein molecules adsorb at the surface, the surface tension decreases significantly to attain a lower equilibrium surface tension. DST curves are sensitive to the adsorption process of proteins. Therefore, the DST measurements can be used to indirectly monitor the adsorption process of proteins. The DST curves of different concentrations of BSA solution at pH 6.0 are shown in Fig. 3 (a). An induction time is observed in the initial DST data at the lowest BSA concentration of 0.1μmol·L−1. Meanwhile, the surface tension of BSA solution (0.1μmol·L−1) attains the mesoequilibrium value (57.76 mN·m−1) until the surface age reaches 10,000s. However, no induction time is observed at the protein concentration of 10 and 1000μmol·L−1. The period of adsorption equilibrium decreases, as the protein concentration increases. For globular proteins, the surface tension significantly decreases from the pure solvent until approximately 50% of the monolayer surface concentration. Therefore, as the BSA concentration increases, the induction time decreases until completely disappears. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the BSA molecules are rapidly adsorbed. Despite their extremely effective surface hydrophilicity value, BSA molecules exhibit a rapid adsorption process, which results from their relatively soft structure [53]. At a protein concentration of 1mmol·L−1, the adsorption attains mesoequilibrium within 1700s. Moreover, the long-term DST decreases with the increase of protein concentration.

The effects of CQDs on the DST of BSA solution at pH 6.0 and 4.3 are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c). At pH 6.0, after the addition of a small amount of CQDs (<0.024g·L−1), the DST curves of BSA/CQD mixtures appear similar to that of the pure BSA solution. At pH 6.0, which is higher than the isoelectric point of BSA, the BSA molecules and CQDs possess similar surface charges (negative charge). Such observation indicates the relatively weak BSA molecules and CQDs interaction, which is also proven by the fluorescence quenching measurements. Moreover, the electrostatic repulsion is unfavorable for the formation of BSA/CQD complex. Therefore, the addition of CQD solution has no significant effect on the adsorption behavior of BSA molecules at lower CQD concentration. Moreover, given the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface, CQDs exhibit extremely high hydrophilicity and almost nil surface activity (the black squares in Fig. 3(b)) [48]. CQDs cannot gradually displace the BSA molecules from the adsorption layers. This condition is different from the adsorption behavior of surfactant–protein mixtures [21]. As a result, the surface adsorption layer is dominated by BSA molecules. In Fig. 2(b), the addition of 0.006g·L−1 of CQDs (red curve) significantly decreases the fluorescence intensity, but the red shift in fluorescence emission is negligible. Therefore, the addition of a small amount of CQDs does not affect protein conformation, and the adsorption properties of BSA molecules remain unchanged. As the CQD concentration further increases, a gradual increase in adsorption rate of proteins is observed, and the mesoequilibrium surface tension decreases. The interaction between BSA and CQDs in the bulk solution induces the conformational change of BSA molecules, which can be proven by the fluorescence quenching measurements (Fig. 2(b)). This conformational change influences the surface properties of BSA molecules. This result is similar to the effects of guanidine hydrochloride on the dynamic surface pressure of BSA and β-lactoglobulin [18]. In general, protein unfolding increases the volume of protein molecule, thus decreasing the diffusion coefficient of protein molecule. However, the denatured protein molecules can spread on the surface and occupy a larger area than the normal protein molecules, resulting in a faster decrease in surface tension [18]. Moreover, at higher CQD concentration, the probability of collision increases, favoring the formation of BSA/CQD complex. The formation of complex and static quenching are generally considered as the interaction mechanism between protein and nanoparticles [51]. In addition, the adsorption behavior of protein on the nanoparticle surface was also extensively investigated in recent years.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), at pH 4.3, which is lower than the isoelectric point of BSA molecules, the effects of CQDs on the DST of BSA solution differ strongly from the corresponding results obtained at pH 6.0. CQDs begin to affect the DST at a concentration as low as 0.006g·L−1. Moreover, the addition of CQDs accelerates the adsorption process of proteins, and the mesoequilibrium surface tension significantly decreases. When the pH value is lower than the isoelectric point, BSA molecules have a positive surface charge. The electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged BSA molecules and CQDs favors the formation of BSA/CQDs complexes. The formation of BSA/CQDs complexes shields the electrostatic repulsion between BSA molecules, thus lowering the electrostatic adsorption barrier. Consequently, the formation of BSA/CQD complexes accelerates the adsorption process of BSA molecules. However, as shown in Fig. 3(c), the DST curves indicate that the adsorption rate and surface activity of BSA/CQD complexes increase slightly further at CQD concentrations above 0.024g·L−1 (violet triangles).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic dilational properties of BSA/CQD mixtures at the air–water interface
ce:section.ce:para: The conformational transitions and composition of protein adsorption layers can be examined using the surface dilational properties [27,54]. The effects of CQDs on the dilational surface elasticity of BSA at pH 6.0 are shown in Fig. 4 (a). For pure proteins, the values of surface dilational modulus are close to those of surface dilational elasticity. The values of surface dilational viscosity can be considered negligible compared with those of surface dilational elasticity, and the phase angles are close to zero [55]. Therefore, the BSA adsorption layers exhibit elasticity. The figures below only show the surface dilational elasticity. The dynamic surface dilational elasticity of pure BSA solution first increases remarkably then slightly decreases as a function of the interface age. The increase in the surface dilational elasticity results from the increasing adsorption amount of proteins. The highest surface dilational elasticity value is approximately 70mN·m−1, which is a characteristic value of globular proteins. When the CQD concentration is lower than 0.024g·L−1, the kinetic dependencies of the surface dilational elasticity of BSA/CQDs mixtures are similar to that of pure BSA solution. Moreover, the dilational elasticity is slightly higher than that of pure BSA solution, indicating that the addition of small amount of CQD solution does not influence the conformation of BSA molecules and the properties of BSA adsorption layer. At CQD concentrations above 0.024g·L−1, the BSA/CQD mixtures change slightly faster in the surface dilational elasticity compared with the pure BSA solution. However, further addition of CQD solution results in an abrupt change in the kinetic dependencies of the dilational elasticity, which peaks at 80min. Meanwhile, the maximum dilational elasticity decreases from 84.54 mN·m−1 to 65.34 mN·m−1. In general, the appearance of a maximum dilational elasticity of proteins suggests the conformational change in the adsorption layer. The destruction of the tertiary and secondary structure of BSA molecules increases the protein flexibility, which favors the formation of loops and tails in the surface adsorption layer [15]. The adsorption layer transforms from an almost two-dimensional surface into a three-dimensional structure. The exchange between the distal and proximal regions of the surface adsorption layer causes a significant decrease in the surface elasticity [25]. Moreover, the conformational destruction of BSA molecules results in a more compact packing, hastening the decrease in surface tension [18]. Therefore, the change in surface dilational elasticity of BSA/CQD solutions significantly accelerates compared with that of the pure BSA solution.

At pH 4.3, when CQD concentrations are lower than 0.018g·L−1, the equilibrium values of surface dilational elasticity increase monotonically with increasing CQD concentration (Fig. 4(b)). Moreover, the surface dilational elasticity of BSA/CQD mixtures increases faster. As mentioned above, at pH 4.3, the formation of BSA/CQD complexes shields the electrostatic repulsion between BSA molecules, thus lowering the electrostatic adsorption barrier. This shielding of the electrostatic adsorption barrier causes the acceleration of protein adsorption. Given the effects of adsorption rate on the dilational elasticity, CQDs also hastens the increase in the dilational elasticity. Moreover, given the conformational transition of BSA molecules, which results from the electrostatic interaction between BSA and CQDs, the adsorbed BSA molecules are more compact in the surface adsorption layer, causing an increase in the surface dilational elasticity of BSA/CQD solution. However, at CQD concentrations higher than 0.012g·L−1, the kinetic dependency of the surface dilational elasticity undergoes a significant transition from the monotonous increase toward the maximum value. Unlike at pH 6.0, this transition occurs at a lower CQD concentration, suggesting a stronger interaction between BSA molecules and CQDs at pH 4.3. Furthermore, the transition indicates the conformational change in the surface adsorption layer. As the CQD concentration increases from 0.018g·L−1 to 0.030g·L−1, the maximal value of surface dilation elasticity monotonously decreases from 64.89mN·m−1 to 45.23mN·m−1. At the CQD concentration higher than 0.030g·L−1, the kinetic dependencies of the surface dilational elasticity of BSA/CQD mixtures are stable. This result indicates that the addition of CQDs cannot further destroy the molecule structure of proteins [15].

The dynamic surface dilational elasticity as a function of surface pressure for the BSA/CQD mixtures at pH 6.0 is shown in Fig. 5 (a). At a low CQD concentration, the surface elasticity of BSA solution monotonically increases with increasing surface pressure and finally attains certain values. However, the addition of CQDs results in a significant transition of kinetic dependence of surface elasticity when the CQD concentration exceeds 0.036g·L−1. The surface elasticity reaches the maximum values at a surface pressure of 16 mN·m−1. As shown in Fig. 5(b), at pH 4.3, the kinetic dependence of surface elasticity transitions at a lower CQD concentration. The surface elasticity reaches the maximum values at the similar surface pressure with pH 6.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CQDs on the DST and dynamic dilational properties of BSA/DPPC system at the air–water interface
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 6 (a), the DST of 0.67g·L−1 DPPC dispersion decreases from ∼70mN·m−1 to ∼45mN·m−1 after 4400s. The decrease in the DST of DPPC dispersion results from the unzipping of vesicles and the formation of DPPC monolayer [56]. Meanwhile, the DST of 10μmol·L−1 BSA reaches ∼55mN·m−1 after 4400s. Compared with that of DPPC, the DST of BSA drops faster within 1000s, suggesting the faster adsorption rate of BSA molecules than DPPC. However, the surface tension of DPPC at 4400s is lower than that of BSA molecules. This condition is attributed to the denser DPPC monolayer, which results from the unzipping of vesicles [31,57]. For the BSA/DPPC mixtures, the DST curve is similar to that of BSA solution, and the surface tension of BSA/DPPC mixtures is slightly higher than that of the BSA solution. Such condition indicates that DPPC and BSA do not have a synergistic effect on surface tension reduction. Moreover, BSA molecules, rather than DPPC, dominate the adsorption process [31]. The preferably adsorbed BSA molecules inhibit the formation of DPPC monolayer. Thus, the surface layer mainly consists of BSA molecules. For BSA/DPPC/CQD mixtures, the DST curve is similar to that of BSA solution but with lower values. Similar to the results in Fig. 3(b), CQDs induce the conformational change of BSA molecules and then accelerate the adsorption rate of BSA molecules. As shown in Fig. 6(b), at the BSA concentration of 0.1μmol·L−1, DPPC controls the surface tension behavior of BSA/DPPC and BSA/DPPC/CQD mixtures. However, the addition of BSA and CQDs slightly increases the surface tension. Therefore, BSA and CQDs can partly inhibit the formation of DPPC monolayer at the surface. Moreover, we detected the influences of CQDs on the long-term surface tension of DPPC. First, when the 0.67g·L−1 DPPC dispersion was equilibrated for 5000s, the dynamic tension reached ∼45 mN·m−1. Then, when the CQD solution of 0.5g·L−1 was introduced in the DPPC dispersion, a new bubble was produced, and the solution was equilibrated for another 5000s. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the long-term surface tension of DPPC/CQD mixtures is higher than that of DPPC dispersion. When the above experiment was replicated, an increase in the long-term surface tension of DPPC/CQD mixtures was observed. DPPC can be adsorbed at the CQD surface, because nanoparticles possess larger surface energy. Therefore, the adsorption of DPPC at the QD surface decreases the surface activity of DPPC.

The surface dilational elasticity of BSA/DPPC and BSA/DPPC/CQDs system at the air–water interface was also determined. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), for 10μmol·L−1 BSA and 0.67g·L−1DPPC mixtures, the dynamic dilation elasticity is similar to that of 10μmol·L−1 BSA solution, indicating that the surface film is dominated by BSA molecules. By contrast, the dynamic surface dilation elasticity of 0.1μmol·L−1 BSA/0.67g·L−1 DPPC mixtures is similar to that of DPPC dispersion (Fig. 7(b)). Thus, DPPC controls the dynamic dilational properties of surface films. Results are consistent with the finding of DST (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction mechanism between CQDs and BSA or BSA/DPPC at the air–water interface
ce:section.ce:para: Results of the fluorescence quenching demonstrate that CQDs decrease the fluorescence intensity of BSA, and causes a red shift in fluorescence emission. Moreover, findings indicate that CQDs interact with BSA proteins and induce the conformational changes of BSA molecules in the bulk phase. According to previous reports, the fluorescence quenching of BSA induced by nanoparticles can be attributed to the formation of a complex (Fig. 8 (a)). The conformational changes and complex formation influence the dynamic adsorption properties of proteins and the dynamic dilation properties of protein adsorption layers [18]. Given the negative surface charges and good hydrophilicity of CQDs, electrostatic interaction is crucial to the interaction between CQDs and BSA molecules. In different pH regions, BSA molecules exhibit different conformations [27]. Here, the interaction of CQDs with BSA of N form and F form was determined. Given that the isoelectric point for BSA molecules is 4.7, BSA with N form exhibits negative surface charges, whereas F form shows positive surface charges. Therefore, the interaction of F form BSA with CQDs is stronger than that of F form BSA, as confirmed by fluorescence quenching experiment.

To acknowledge the unfolding process of BSA at the surface, Noskov et al. investigated the kinetic dependencies of surface pressure and dilational surface elasticity of BSA at different pH values [18]. The researchers concluded that the kinetic dependency of BSA with F form is similar to that of BSA with N form. Unlike non-globular proteins, BSA (globular protein) exhibits a high dilational surface elasticity (∼75mN·m−1), and the kinetic dependence of BSA is monotonous (type A, Fig. 4) [15]. This condition indicates that BSA molecules can maintain the native conformation in the adsorption process. By contrast, the kinetic dependence of surface elasticity of non-globular proteins exhibits one or two maximum values (type B) [15]. At low CQD concentration, the surface elasticity of BSA solution continues to monotonically increase as the surface age increases. However, the addition of CQDs results in a significant transition from type A to type B when the CQD concentration exceeds a critical value. Moreover, the critical CQD concentration at pH 4.3 is lower than that the value obtained at pH 6.0. Results are consistent with the findings in investigating the effects of denaturant on the surface elasticity of BSA solutions [19]. Given the irreversible adsorption of BSA molecules at the surface, the main relaxation process at the surface dilation and compression occurs inside the protein adsorption layer [58]. The appearance of the maximum surface elasticity values can be attributed to the formation of tails and loops [18]. When protein molecules lost their secondary and tertiary structures, the dilational elasticity of the protein adsorption layer can be analyzed using the models for linear flexible polymer. The protein adsorption layer usually consists of two zones: the proximal and distal regions (Fig. 8(a)) [15]. When the surface pressure exceeds 16mN·m−1, some hydrophilic parts of protein molecules are squeezed out of the proximal region as loops and tails to the distal region. The rapid exchange between proximal and distal regions results in the decrease in the surface elasticity [25]. As the CQD concentration increases, the conformation changes in BSA molecules induced by CQDs probably help the formation of loops and tails.

As shown in Fig. 8(b), the dynamic surface properties are dominated by BSA molecules because of the faster adsorption rate of BSA molecules than DPPC [31]. Therefore, the effects of CQDs on the surface properties of DPPC/BSA mixture are similar to those of BSA alone. In Fig. 8(c), when BSA concentration is far below DPPC concentration, the dynamic surface properties are controlled by DPPC molecules. The decrease in the DST of DPPC dispersion results from the unzipping of vesicles and the formation of DPPC monolayer. The addition of CQDs solution can partly inhibit the formation of DPPC monolayer at the air-water interface. This inhibition can be attributed to the interaction of DPPC with CQDs, which results in the hydrophobization of CQDs surface. The hydrophobic nanoparticles will block a part of the DPPC monolayer. The surface layer mainly consists of DPPC molecules and hydrophobic nanoparticles. This non-uniform surface is defined as composite surface [59]. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the dilational elasticity of DPPC/CQDs composite surface is lower than that of uniform DPPC monolayer.

Results of the fluorescence quenching demonstrate that CQDs decrease the fluorescence intensity of BSA and cause a red shift in fluorescence emission. The quenching constant at pH 4.3 is twice as large as that of the value obtained at pH 6.0. The addition of CQDs induces the conformational change of BSA molecules in the bulk phase. The tryptophan residues previously buried in a hydrophobic region are exposed to a more hydrophilic environment. At pH 6.0, the DST curves of BSA/CQD mixtures are similar to those of the pure BSA solution at low CQD concentrations (<0.024g·L−1). As the CQD concentration increases, a gradual increase in adsorption rate of proteins and decrease in mesoequilibrium surface tension are observed. This conformational change influences the interfacial properties of BSA molecules. At pH 4.3, CQDs begin to affect the DST at a concentration as low as 0.006g·L−1. The addition of CQDs results in a significant transition of kinetic dependencies of surface elasticity of BSA solution when the CQD concentration exceeds a critical value. The critical CQD concentration at pH 4.3 is lower than the value obtained at pH 6.0. The appearance of the maximum surface elasticity value can be attributed to the formation of tails and loops. When surface properties are dominated by BSA molecules, the addition of CQDs has little effect on the DPPC/BSA mixture surface properties, which are similar to that of BSA alone. However, when the surface film mainly consists of DPPC, CQDs can partly inhibit the formation of DPPC monolayer at the air–water interface, which can be attributed to the interaction of DPPC with CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant nos. 21403017, 21473125), the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (grant no. 21225313), and the Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi.
